perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information " shall mean all confidential or proprietary written, recorded, electronic or oral information or data (including without limitation research, developmental, engineering, manufacturing, technical, marketing, sales, financial, operating, performance, cost, business and process information or data, trade secrets, discoveries, ideas, designs, data, source code, object code, processes, computer programs, developments, flow diagrams, know-how, and computer programming and other software and software techniques) provided (whether such confidentiality or proprietary status is indicated orally or in writing, whether or not the specific words "confidential" or "proprietary" are used) to a Party (the “Receiving Party”) by the other Party (the “Disclosing Party”) in the course of the exchange of such information or data between the Parties. Without limiting the aforesaid, the existence of discussions between the Parties regarding the Proposed Transaction shall constitute Confidential Information hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: "Confidential Information" shall initially mean all confidential or proprietary written, recorded, electronic or oral information or data (including without limitation research, developmental, engineering, manufacturing, technical, marketing, sales, financial, operating, performance, cost, business and process information or data, trade secrets, discoveries, ideas, designs, data, source code, object code, processes, computer programs, developments, flow diagrams, know-how, and computer programming and other software and software techniques) provided (whether such confidentiality or proprietary status is indicated orally or in writing, whether or not the specific words "confidential" or "proprietary" are used) to a Party (the “Receiving Party”) by the other Party (the “Disclosing Party”) in the course of the exchange of such information or data between the Parties. However, after six months from the date of this agreement, unless the Disclosing Party provides written notice to the Receiving Party, 'Confidential Information' will be limited to only trade secrets and source code.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the phrase 'However, after six months from the date of this agreement, unless the Disclosing Party provides written notice to the Receiving Party, 'Confidential Information' will be limited to only trade secrets and source code,' we introduce an in-text contradiction. Initially, 'Confidential Information' is broadly defined but after six months, it narrows significantly unless an action is taken. This contradicts the subsequent sections of the agreement that refer to 'Confidential Information' without specifying if it's before or after the six-month period, especially Section 2, which mandates continuous confidentiality for all 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party shall hold confidential and not disclose to any Person, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, all Confidential Information and any information about the Proposed Transaction, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives; provided, however, that the Receiving Party may disclose such Confidential Information to its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in its evaluation of the Proposed Transaction or who otherwise need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of evaluating the Proposed Transaction;
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Confidential Information' to have a time-sensitive component, narrowing its scope after six months unless a specific action is taken. The contradicted text, Section 2, imposes a continuous and unqualified obligation to maintain the confidentiality of all 'Confidential Information.' This creates a direct contradiction because after six months, certain information initially classified as 'Confidential Information' no longer falls under that definition without written notice, yet Section 2 continues to mandate its confidentiality, leading to ambiguity and potential unenforceability. This aligns with the definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction because the meaning and obligations tied to 'Confidential Information' are inconsistently applied.

perturbation.original_text: “ Representatives ” shall mean as to any Person, its directors, officers, employees, agents and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, attorneys and accountants).
perturbation.changed_text: “Representatives” shall mean as to any Person, its directors, officers, and employees. Financial advisors, attorneys, and accountants shall only be considered 'Representatives' if they are directly employed by the Person.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the definition of 'Representatives' by excluding external advisors unless they are directly employed. This is an in-text contradiction because Section 2.b states 'The Receiving Party shall cause all its Representatives to observe the terms of this Agreement and shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by it or its Representatives;' with the new definition, the Receiving Party is not responsible for the breach of terms made by Financial advisors, attorneys, and accountants, unless they are directly employed by the Person.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party shall cause all its Representatives to observe the terms of this Agreement and shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by it or its Representatives; and
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Representatives' to exclude external advisors unless directly employed. This contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the Receiving Party is responsible for any breach of the agreement by its Representatives. With the new definition, the Receiving Party is no longer responsible for breaches by financial advisors, attorneys, and accountants who are not directly employed, creating a direct conflict and inconsistency in the document as it relates to the scope of responsibility.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall return or destroy all Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) within thirty (30) days of receipt of a written request.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall return or destroy all Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) within thirty (30) days of receipt of a written request, unless the Receiving Party reasonably believes such Confidential Information is necessary for ongoing compliance with regulatory requirements.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'unless the Receiving Party reasonably believes such Confidential Information is necessary for ongoing compliance with regulatory requirements', the obligation to return or destroy the information becomes conditional and creates uncertainty. This change contradicts Section 6 that states 'Unless sooner terminated by mutual written Agreement of the Parties hereto, this Agreement and the obligations hereunder shall terminate one (1) year from the date hereof.' because even after termination of agreement obligations can last longer.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Unless sooner terminated by mutual written Agreement of the Parties hereto, this Agreement and the obligations hereunder shall terminate one (1) year from the date hereof.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, making it conditional on the Receiving Party's belief about regulatory compliance. This contradicts the original 'Unless sooner terminated...' clause, which states that all obligations terminate after one year. The added exception creates a scenario where obligations regarding confidential information could extend beyond the one-year termination date, creating an in-text contradiction as explained by the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations imposed by Section 2 hereof shall not apply, or shall cease to apply, to any Confidential Information if or when, but only to the extent that, such Confidential Information: a. was known to the Receiving Party prior to the receipt of the Confidential Information; or b. was, or becomes through no breach of the Receiving Party's obligations hereunder, known to the public; or c. becomes known to the Receiving Party from sources other than the Disclosing Party under circumstances not involving any breach of any confidentiality obligation; or d. is independently developed by the Receiving Party, as evidenced by the written records thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations imposed by Section 2 hereof shall not apply, or shall cease to apply, to any Confidential Information if or when, but only to the extent that, such Confidential Information: a. was known to the Receiving Party prior to the receipt of the Confidential Information and the Receiving Party has documented proof of such prior knowledge; or b. was, or becomes through no breach of the Receiving Party's obligations hereunder, known to the public due to the Disclosing Party’s actions; or c. becomes known to the Receiving Party from sources other than the Disclosing Party under circumstances not involving any breach of any confidentiality obligation, and such source is a government entity; or d. is independently developed by the Receiving Party, as evidenced by the written records thereof, and the Disclosing Party approves such development.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces stricter conditions for the exceptions to confidentiality. Requiring 'documented proof,' 'due to the Disclosing Party's actions', 'such source is a government entity' and 'the Disclosing Party approves such development' respectively, contradicts the original intent of Section 3, which provided clearer and less stringent exceptions. Section 2 states that Receiving Party should not disclose any information to any person without the prior written consent of the disclosing party but Section 3.d requires disclosing party approve independent development.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party shall hold confidential and not disclose to any Person, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, all Confidential Information and any information about the Proposed Transaction, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Confidential Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives; provided, however, that the Receiving Party may disclose such Confidential Information to its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in its evaluation of the Proposed Transaction or who otherwise need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of evaluating the Proposed Transaction;
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds conditions to the exceptions outlined in Section 3 concerning confidential information. These additions, such as requiring 'documented proof,' needing the disclosure to be 'due to the Disclosing Party's actions', requiring the source to be a 'government entity,' and mandating 'the Disclosing Party approves such development,' create stricter and narrower exceptions than originally intended. This contradicts the obligation in the 'Contradicted Text' section which states that the Receiving Party shall not disclose any information without prior written consent from the Disclosing Party, but Section 3.d now requires disclosing party approval, indicating an in-text contradiction according to the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'. The modifications introduce ambiguity and stricter constraints where the original text expected broader allowances, creating inconsistency in enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall be free at all times to hold negotiations or enter into agreements with any other persons whatsoever (including with respect to projects under discussion by the Parties hereto) in addition to or in lieu of the discussions hereunder and any such activities shall not be a breach of this agreement or any obligations owed to the other Party hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall be free at all times to hold negotiations or enter into agreements with any other persons whatsoever (excluding any direct competitors of the other party) in addition to or in lieu of the discussions hereunder and any such activities shall not be a breach of this agreement or any obligations owed to the other Party hereunder. However, the Parties shall not use the Confidential Information to directly compete with the Disclosing Party for a period of 2 years after the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This revision restricts the freedom to negotiate with 'direct competitors' and introduces a non-compete clause lasting two years after termination. This directly contradicts the original statement in Section 4, 'The Parties shall be free at all times', making it ambiguous under what circumstances negotiations are permitted. This clause contradicts Section 6: Termination; Duration of Obligations, the termination clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Unless sooner terminated by mutual written Agreement of the Parties hereto, this Agreement and the obligations hereunder shall terminate one (1) year from the date hereof.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a restriction on negotiating with direct competitors and a non-compete clause, which directly conflicts with the contradicted text stating that the agreement terminates after one year. The changed text states that parties cannot compete for two years post-termination, which means obligations extend beyond the one-year termination stated in the contradicted text. This is a clear contradiction because the changed text imposes obligations beyond the termination date specified in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement represents the entire understanding and agreement of the Parties and supersedes all prior communications, agreements and understandings between the Parties relating to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement represents the entire understanding and agreement of the Parties as of the Effective Date and supersedes all prior communications, agreements and understandings between the Parties relating to the subject matter hereof. However, any pre-existing confidentiality agreements between the Parties pertaining to specific technologies discussed during the Proposed Transaction shall remain in full force and effect.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'However, any pre-existing confidentiality agreements between the Parties pertaining to specific technologies discussed during the Proposed Transaction shall remain in full force and effect' we carve out an exception to the 'entire agreement' clause. This contradicts the main clause of section 7 since now pre-existing confidentiality agreements can still be valid. This causes ambiguity on which agreement stands.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement represents the entire understanding and agreement of the Parties and supersedes all prior communications, agreements and understandings between the Parties relating to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the agreement supersedes all prior agreements. The changed text adds an exception for pre-existing confidentiality agreements. This creates a direct conflict with the original intent of the 'entire agreement' clause as captured in the contradicted text, making it ambiguous which agreements are valid. This aligns with the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition, specifically when key obligations are defined inconsistently, making interpretation unclear.
